schools.summary: Chrysostom: Titus was an approved one of the companions of Paul; otherwise he would not have committed to him the charge of that whole island (Crete), nor commanded him to supply what was deficient ('that thou shouldest set in order the things that are wanting'), nor given him jurisdiction over so many bishops; Paul opening as a servant of God and apostle of Jesus Christ, according to the faith of God's elect and the acknowledging of the truth which is after godliness — the apostleship aimed at the elect's faith and a truth that breeds godliness (Paul naming himself 'a servant of God,' bound to his service, and 'an apostle of Jesus Christ,' sent by his authority, and stating the end of his office: 'according to the faith of God's elect,' to bring the chosen to faith, 'and the acknowledging of the truth which is after godliness,' the truth that is no barren notion but accords with and produces godliness, so the very salutation marks the gospel as a truth that always tends to holy living).

themes: Paul, a servant of God, and an apostle of Jesus Christ
according to the faith of God's elect
the acknowledging of the truth which is after godliness
the gospel a truth that produces godliness

schools.summary: Gill: 'in hope of eternal life' (or 'for the hope of eternal life,' in order to bring souls to the hope of it) is another end of the gospel ministry — as to bring God's elect to faith in Christ and to the knowledge and acknowledgement of the truth, so to the hope of eternal glory and happiness; and this hope rests on the promise of God, who cannot lie; Paul declaring that this faith and truth are in hope of eternal life, which God who cannot lie promised before the world began — the sure hope grounded in the eternal promise of the truthful God (Paul naming the great object toward which his ministry leads, 'the hope of eternal life,' and giving the firmest possible ground for that hope: it rests on what 'God, that cannot lie,' the God whose very nature forbids falsehood, 'promised before the world began,' in the eternal counsel and covenant from everlasting, so the believer's hope of glory stands not on a changing thing but on the unbreakable word of the God who cannot lie, given before time itself).

themes: in hope of eternal life
which God, that cannot lie
promised before the world began
hope resting on the unbreakable word of God

schools.summary: Barnes: 'in due times' means at the proper time, the time which God had intended, the best time; Gill: God has manifested either Christ his essential Word, or the word of truth the gospel, or rather his word of promise of eternal life in Christ Jesus; Paul declaring that God has in due times manifested his word through preaching, which was committed to him by the commandment of God our Saviour — the eternal promise now openly published through the preaching entrusted to Paul (Paul showing how the ancient promise has come to light: God 'hath in due times manifested his word,' what was hidden in his eternal counsel he has at the appointed and best season made openly known, and that 'through preaching,' the public proclamation of the gospel, which proclamation 'is committed unto me' as a sacred trust 'according to the commandment of God our Saviour,' so the everlasting purpose is now made manifest by the preached word, the very office laid on Paul by God's own command).

themes: but hath in due times manifested his word through preaching
which is committed unto me
according to the commandment of God our Saviour
the eternal promise published through preaching

schools.summary: Gill: Titus was Paul's own son not in a natural but a spiritual sense, the apostle being the instrument of his conversion (as of Onesimus and many of the Corinthians, whom therefore he is said to beget, being their spiritual father), and 'after the common faith,' the faith common to all the elect, Jew and Gentile alike; Paul addressing Titus as his own son after the common faith, with grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ — the affectionate address and full blessing to a fellow-worker (Paul greeting Titus as 'mine own son,' a true spiritual son converted under his ministry, and 'after the common faith,' in the one faith shared by all believers who are equally the elect of God, and invoking the threefold blessing of 'grace, mercy, and peace' from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ 'our Saviour,' the fuller benediction with 'mercy' fitting for a minister, all flowing from the Father and the Son together).

themes: to Titus, mine own son after the common faith
grace, mercy, and peace
from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ our Saviour
the spiritual sonship and full minister's blessing

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the whole life of men in ancient times was one of action and contention, while ours is a life of indolence; they knew they were brought into the world to labour according to the will of him who brought them into it, but we live as if placed here only to eat and drink; Paul declaring that he left Titus in Crete to set in order the things wanting and ordain elders in every city — the commission to complete and organize the Cretan churches (Paul recalling the purpose for which he had left Titus on the island: not to rest but with a working charge 'that thou shouldest set in order the things that are wanting,' to put right and complete what was yet lacking in the newly-planted churches, 'and ordain elders in every city,' appoint qualified pastors and overseers in each place, 'as I had appointed thee,' so Titus's task was the great work of organizing, completing, and providing settled leadership for the churches).

themes: for this cause left I thee in Crete
that thou shouldest set in order the things that are wanting
and ordain elders in every city
the commission to organize the churches

schools.summary: Gill: he must be blameless in his outward life and conversation, not chargeable with any notorious crime; Matthew Henry: the apostle here gives Titus directions about ordination, showing whom he should ordain and whom not, pointing out the qualifications and virtues of those to be ordained; Paul setting the elder's qualifications — blameless, the husband of one wife, having faithful well-ordered children — the proven character and ordered home required of an elder (Paul, beginning the list of qualifications for those Titus is to ordain, requiring first that the man be 'blameless,' of unblemished reputation against whom no just charge can be brought, 'the husband of one wife,' faithful and chaste in marriage, not a polygamist or of loose life, and well-ordered in his own home, 'having faithful children not accused of riot or unruly,' children who are believing and orderly rather than wild or rebellious, so the elder must be proved in his own character and household before he is set over God's house).

themes: if any be blameless, the husband of one wife
having faithful children
not accused of riot or unruly
proven in character and home before ruling

schools.summary: Gill: this shows that a bishop and an elder is the same (the Syriac here renders it 'an elder'); the character or qualification necessary is the same as before and to be understood in like manner, unless it refer more particularly to his faithfulness in the discharge of his office as the steward of God's house; Paul requiring that a bishop be blameless as God's steward — not selfwilled, not soon angry, not given to wine, no striker, not greedy of filthy lucre — the bishop a steward who must be free from the vices that disqualify (Paul, identifying the elder with the 'bishop' or overseer, grounding the demand for blamelessness in his office: he is 'the steward of God,' entrusted to manage God's household, and so must be free of a list of disqualifying faults, 'not selfwilled,' not arrogant and self-pleasing, 'not soon angry,' not quick-tempered, 'not given to wine,' no drunkard, 'no striker,' not violent or quarrelsome, 'not given to filthy lucre,' not greedy for base gain, so the man who manages God's house must himself be free of these blemishes).

themes: for a bishop must be blameless, as the steward of God
not selfwilled, not soon angry
not given to wine, no striker, not given to filthy lucre
the steward free of disqualifying vices

schools.summary: Barnes: 'a lover of good' (philagathos) means a lover of good and may apply to anything that is good — it may refer to good men as included under the general term good, and there is none more essential to a minister than this; Paul requiring that the bishop be a lover of hospitality, a lover of good, sober, just, holy, and temperate — the positive graces that must adorn the overseer (Paul, having listed the faults the bishop must lack, naming the virtues he must possess: 'a lover of hospitality,' gladly welcoming and entertaining strangers and saints, 'a lover of good men,' or of good in every form, loving what is good, 'sober,' self-controlled and discreet, 'just,' righteous in dealings with men, 'holy,' devout toward God, and 'temperate,' master of his appetites and passions, so the overseer is to be adorned with the whole circle of graces, godward, manward, and self-ward).

themes: but a lover of hospitality, a lover of good men
sober, just, holy, temperate
the positive graces of the overseer
virtues godward, manward, and self-ward

schools.summary: Gill: the 'faithful word' is the doctrine of the gospel, so called because it is true and to be believed — it is the word of truth and truth itself, contains nothing but truth, never deceived any who gave credit to its doctrines and promises, and is pure, unmixed, and unadulterated; Paul requiring that the elder hold fast the faithful word, that by sound doctrine he may both exhort and convince the gainsayers — the doctrinal soundness that equips the elder to teach and refute (Paul crowning the qualifications with the elder's grasp of truth: he must be 'holding fast the faithful word as he hath been taught,' firmly retaining the sound apostolic doctrine, and that to a double end, 'that he may be able by sound doctrine both to exhort,' to encourage and build up the faithful with wholesome teaching, 'and to convince the gainsayers,' to refute and silence those who contradict the truth, so the elder is to be mighty in the Scriptures, both to feed the flock and to stop the mouths of error).

themes: holding fast the faithful word as he hath been taught
that he may be able by sound doctrine both to exhort
and to convince the gainsayers
mighty in Scripture to feed and refute

schools.summary: Gill: there were many unruly persons, who would not submit to the law of God or the gospel of Christ, whose spirits were not subject to the prophets, who would not submit themselves to those who had the rule over them, nor attend to the admonitions of the church, nor be brought into any regularity and order, and there were many such; Paul declaring that there are many unruly, vain talkers, and deceivers, especially those of the circumcision — the reason sound elders are needed, the many deceivers abroad (Paul giving the urgent reason why the elder must be able to convince gainsayers: 'for there are many' such adversaries, 'unruly,' insubordinate and refusing all order and authority, 'vain talkers,' full of empty idle words, and 'deceivers,' leading souls astray, and 'specially they of the circumcision,' the Judaizing teachers who pressed the law and Jewish observances on the churches, so the abundance and danger of these errorists makes sound, capable elders the more necessary).

themes: for there are many unruly and vain talkers and deceivers
specially they of the circumcision
the many deceivers needing refutation
the Judaizers making sound elders necessary

schools.summary: Gill: their mouths must be stopped, that is, they must be silenced by reasons and arguments fetched out of the word of God, as the Sadducees and Pharisees were silenced by Christ so that they durst ask him no more questions, and as the Jews at Damascus were silenced by Saul, who confounded them, proving clearly that Jesus was the Christ; Paul charging that the deceivers' mouths be stopped, since they subvert whole houses, teaching what they ought not for filthy lucre's sake — the silencing of greedy deceivers who ruin households (Paul showing what is to be done with these errorists and why: their 'mouths must be stopped,' silenced by sound argument from the Scripture, refuted and put to shame, because 'they subvert whole houses,' overturn the faith of entire families, 'teaching things which they ought not,' spreading doctrines they have no right to teach, and that 'for filthy lucre's sake,' from a base motive of greed, so that, being both destructive and mercenary, these teachers must be effectually refuted and stopped).

themes: whose mouths must be stopped
who subvert whole houses, teaching things which they ought not
for filthy lucre's sake
silencing destructive, mercenary deceivers

schools.summary: Chrysostom: several questions arise here — who said this, why Paul quoted it, and why he brings forward a testimony from a heathen poet; Paul, as when he was discoursing to the Athenians and in the course of his harangue cited their own poets ('for we are also his offspring'), here turns the Cretans' own witness against them; Paul quoting a Cretan prophet of their own that the Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, and lazy gluttons — the Cretans' own poet witnessing to their bad character (Paul, to justify the sharp dealing the Cretan churches required, citing the testimony of 'one of themselves, even a prophet of their own,' the Cretan poet Epimenides esteemed among them as a seer, who had branded his own countrymen: 'The Cretians are alway liars, evil beasts, slow bellies,' habitual liars, fierce and brutish in manners, and lazy gluttonous idlers; Paul's wisdom in citing a pagan poet's own word, turning the nation's reputation, confirmed by their own authority, to show why a sharp rebuke was needed).

debates.question: Why does Paul quote a heathen Cretan poet?

sides.position: To turn the Cretans' own authority against them — citing their esteemed poet's testimony to their bad character (as he cited the Athenians' poets at the Areopagus), confirming why a sharp rebuke was needed

themes: one of themselves, even a prophet of their own
the Cretians are alway liars, evil beasts, slow bellies
the Cretans' own poet witnessing against them
turning their own authority to justify rebuke

schools.summary: Gill: Paul confirms what the poet had said — he knew it to be fact from his own experience and the observation he had made when in the island; he does not say that all Epimenides said in the poem was true, but this character which he had given of the Cretians was; Paul affirming that this witness is true and charging that they be rebuked sharply, that they may be sound in the faith — the sharp rebuke aimed at making the erring sound (Paul, endorsing the poet's verdict, 'this witness is true,' confirmed by his own observation of the Cretans, drawing the practical charge: 'wherefore rebuke them sharply,' deal with such hardened, faulty characters not gently but with cutting severity as their case requires, and that with a healing aim, 'that they may be sound in the faith,' so the sharpness is no cruelty but a surgeon's, meant to cure the disease of error and bring them to soundness).

themes: this witness is true
wherefore rebuke them sharply
that they may be sound in the faith
sharp rebuke with a healing aim

schools.summary: Gill: the Jewish fables concerned God himself, the angels, the creation of man, the giving of the law at Mount Sinai, the Messiah and his earthly kingdom, and the feast that would be made for the righteous in his days (of flesh, fish, and fowl, Behemoth, Leviathan, and the like) — idle traditions of the elders; Paul charging that they give no heed to Jewish fables and the commandments of men that turn from the truth — the rejection of Jewish myth and man-made rule (Paul naming what the Cretan believers, made sound in the faith, must shun: 'Jewish fables,' the idle myths and traditions of the rabbis, 'and commandments of men,' human rules and impositions not God's, both of which 'turn from the truth,' lead the soul away from the true gospel, so soundness in the faith means refusing both the fanciful legends and the man-made commandments by which the Judaizers drew men from Christ).

themes: not giving heed to Jewish fables
and commandments of men
that turn from the truth
refusing both legend and man-made rule

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle, having mentioned Jewish fables and the traditions of the elders, takes notice of some darling notions these Judaizing Christians had imbibed or retained — that there were some things which, being touched, handled, or tasted, occasioned uncleanness; against this he sets the truth; Paul declaring that to the pure all things are pure, but to the defiled and unbelieving nothing is pure, their very mind and conscience being defiled — the seat of purity and pollution in the heart not in outward things (Paul overthrowing the Judaizers' ceremonial scruples with a great principle: 'unto the pure all things are pure,' to the man whose heart is cleansed by faith all God's creatures, foods and the like, are clean and lawful to use, but 'unto them that are defiled and unbelieving is nothing pure,' for the defilement is not in the thing but in the man, their unbelief and inward corruption tainting everything they touch, since 'even their mind and conscience is defiled,' so purity and pollution lie not in meats or ceremonies but in the heart).

debates.question: Where does purity or defilement lie — in things, or in the heart?

sides.position: In the heart, not in outward things — to the pure (cleansed by faith) all God's creatures are clean and lawful, while to the defiled and unbelieving nothing is pure, their very mind and conscience corrupted

themes: unto the pure all things are pure
but unto them that are defiled and unbelieving is nothing pure
but even their mind and conscience is defiled
purity in the heart, not in things

schools.summary: Gill: these persons professed that they knew God — that there is a God, one only true and living God, the God of Israel as professed by the Jews, and (as Christians) Father, Son, and Spirit, for they were judaizing Christians — yet this knowledge was but notional, contradicted by their lives; Paul declaring that they profess to know God but in works deny him, being abominable, disobedient, and unto every good work reprobate — the empty profession contradicted by an ungodly life (Paul exposing the deepest fault of these defiled teachers: their religion all in word, none in deed, 'they profess that they know God,' claiming the knowledge of the true God, 'but in works they deny him,' their conduct flatly contradicting their creed, and branding their character, 'abominable,' detestable in God's sight, 'disobedient,' rebellious against his will, and 'unto every good work reprobate,' worthless and disqualified for any good deed, so a profession of God belied by an ungodly life is the very mark of these hypocrites).

themes: they profess that they know God; but in works they deny him
being abominable, and disobedient
and unto every good work reprobate
empty profession belied by an ungodly life
